Dear Ms. Fishburn: 
On November 28, 1983, Merle Hall, Council Member of the City of 
Walnut Creek, requested advice from the Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
("initial request"). 
That request sought the 
Commission's advice on (among other issues) the extent to which 
Council Member Hall was entitled to participate in decisions to 
revise the City's "Core Area Plan," the general plan for the 
downtown area of Walnut Creek. 
At the time the request was made the 
revision process was just beginning, and was expected to last 18 
months. 
The Commission therefore replied, in part, as follows: 
At this early stage, there is no indica-
tion of the types of revisions that will 
be made 
and it is not reasonably 
foreseeable that the Council's decision 
to begin the review will have a material 
financial effect on Mr. Hall's interests. 
* * * 
As the revision process continues I 
the 
City Council will have opportunities to 
comment on the Plan's progress and, 
ultimately, it will approve the new Plan. 
It appears likely that Mr. Hall's 
interests will be materially affected by 
some of the future decisions concerning 
the revisions to the Core Area Plan. 
Mr. 
Hall must disqualify himself from these 
decisions I 
unless the effect on his 
interests will be substantially similar 
to the effect on the public generally 
(Your Advice Letter No 
A-83-266, January 
5, 1984, p. 5).

The revision process is now nearing completion; Council Member Hall 
requests the Commission's advice once again concerning his obli-
gations under Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974. 
The facts presented are as follows: 
I. 
STATEMENT OF FACTS. 
Our initial request described in some detail the pertinent 
characteristics of Walnut Creek and the process that is legally 
required for amendments to the 1975 Core Area Plan (p. 1-4). 
Rather 
than repeat those points here, I will simply state what has occurred 
since November, 1983. 
On March 25, 1985, after numerous study sessions and public 
hearings, the Walnut Creek Planning Co~~ission recommended that the 
City Council adopt a revised 1985 Core Area Plan (See Walnut Creek 
Planning Commission Resolutions Nos. 
2342 and 2343, 
and the 
reconunended Plan, all enclosed with this letter. 
The Planning 
Commission certified a three-volume Environmental Impact Report at 
the same time. 
It is not enclosed, but is available upon the 
Commission's request.) 
The proposed Plan consists of five related 
parts: 
1. 
A land use element; 
2. 
A transportation element; 
3. 
An urban design elementi 
4. 
An implementation sectioni and 
5. 
Area plans for four particular areas. 
Following is a brief discussion of the function and significant 
provisions of each section of the proposed Plan. 
1. 
Land Use Element. 
Together with the land use maps, this element specifies the 
permitted use, and permitted intensity of development, of every 
parcel of land in the Core Area. 
The proposed Plan would change the 
permitted uses on many parcels. 
It would also impose, for the first 
time, a floor-area ratio (FAR) on commercial parcels to control the 
intensity of permissible development (Figure 5). 
The floor-area 
ratio of any parcel is the ratio of building floor area to net land 
area t 
both expressed in square feet. 
For a 30,000 square- foot 
parcel, for example an FAR of 1.0 would permit a building floor area 
of 30,000 square feet; an FAR of .5 would allow a building floor 
area of 15,000 square feet; and an FAR of 1.5 would permit a 45,000 
square-foot building. 
Generally speaking, the proposed Plan permits 
less development throughout the Core Area than would have been 
permitted under the 1975 Plan. 


Ms. Diane Fishburn 
June II, 1985 
Page 3 
2. 
Transportation Element. 
The text of this element recites that it 
•.. emerged as the most difficult and 
challenging issue of the Plan revision 
process. 
A wide range of alternatives 
have been explored for both new street 
systems and ways of managing our existing 
transportation resources. 
As Walnut 
Creek approaches the limits of its 
existing street network, there are fewer 
viable courses of action available (p. 
17) 
The Environmental Impact Report noted that many intersections in the 
Core Area already function below acceptable service levels, and that 
service can be expected to deteriorate further because of new 
development inside and outside the Core Area. 
Planning staff there-
fore identified relationships between development capacity generally 
in the Core Area, and the transportation system improvements neces-
sary to maintain adequate service levels when such development 
occurs. 
Even if no development were permitted, however, traffic 
experts predicted that service levels at some intersections would 
continue to deteriorate because of traffic generated by development 
occurring outside the city limits. 
With this background, the Transportation Element calls for 
various measures to maintain and improve pedestrian, bicycle and 
vehicle circulation in the Core Area. 
Central to the element is a 
set of recownended street improvements which range in significance 
from restriping existing roads to the widening of existing streets 
and the construction of new ones (Figure 10). 
The most significant 
debate at the Planning Commission concerned the proper method to 
relieve peak hour congestion on Ygnacio Valley Road, the City's 
major east-west arterial. 
Staff recommended that westbound traffic 
on Ygnacio Valley Road in the Core Area be diverted to a new one-way 
street to be constructed along a new right-of-way; the parallel 
section of ygnacio Valley Road would then be converted to a one-way 
street for eastbound traffic only, with the two streets forming a 
one-way "couplet." 
This approach was rejected by a majority of the 
Planning Commission. 
In place of the couplet, the Plan recommends 
widening of Ygnacio Valley Road to eight lanes in the downtown area. 
Although Ygnacio Valley Road is the most significant 
transportation improvement, I should emphasize that the Plan calls 
for many other street improvements and changes 
transportation 
pol 
to solve local and 
problems. 


Ms. Diane Fishburn 
June II, 1985 
Page 4 
3. 
Urban Design Element. 
This element establishes policies to further the City's 
urban design goals in the Core Area. 
To preserve views and insure a 
proper scale of development, height limits are imposed on all Core 
Area parcels (Figure 13). 
The proposed Plan does not call for any 
increases in building height permissible under the 1975 plan. 
Indeed, height limits for most parcels would be decreased. 
The 
Urban Design Element also establishes building setback lines on 
important streets (Figure 14). 
4. 
Implementation Section. 
This section of the proposed Plan sets forth the measures 
needed to implement the plan's policies. 
These measures include 
capi tal improvements I 
transportation system management programs, 
zoning ordinance changes and studies. 
It also identifies potential 
redevelopment areas. 
5. 
Area Plans. 
The proposed Plan calls out four smaller parts of the Core 
Area for special "area plans." 
Among these areas is the Mt. Diablo 
Boulevard entry, 
for which a 
median and various sidewalk 
improvements are recommended. 
When the draft Core Area Plan was transmitted to the City 
Council by the Planning Co~nission, Council Member Hall assessed the 
impact of the proposed policies on his financial interests. He con-
cluded that, as applied to his property on Mt. Diablo Boulevard, the 
floor area ratio requirements of the Land Use Element and the height 
limit set forth set forth in the Urban Design Element combined to 
reduce the value of his property by approximately $1,000,000. 
We 
discussed at some length the possibility that Council Member Hall 
might participate in discussions and decision on the Transportation 
Element of the Plan, on the theory that the policies set forth in 
that element do not affect his financial interest. 
As the issues 
were framed by the Planning Commission, however, the constraints on 
transportation were the principal justification for limiting 
development intensity throughout the Core Area. 
Thus, at least as 
viewed by the Planning Commission, the issues of transportation and 
intensity of development were inextricably intertwined, with 
transportation the most fundamental. 
Based on these discussions I 
Council Member Hall concluded that he should abstain from official 
participation in the deliberations and decision on the Core Area 
Plan, and he did so. 
During the subsequent public hearing before 
the City Council, Council Member Hall testified as a member of the 
general public before the City Council on the effect of the plan on 
his financial interests, in accordance with 2 Cal.Admin.Code 
§18700 (f) (1) . 


Ms. Diane Fishburn 
June II, 1985 
Page 5 
Since the City Council closed the public hearing on the 
Core Area Plan on May 14, 1985, the Council has deliberated over 
several sessions on the content of the plan. 
For two reasons, the 
City Council decided to take up the Land Use and Urban Design 
Elements first, before beginning its deliberations on the Transpor-
tation Element. 
The Council desired to reach a concensus on these 
elements before the effective date of the resignation of Council 
Member Margaret "Peg" Kovar; the Council also expressed the belief 
that the Land Use and Urban Design Elements are fundamental to 
transportation issues, and that transportation policy should there-
fore be dictated by the contents of the Land Use and Urban Design 
Elements. 
The Council has now concluded its deliberations on those 
elements, and intends to take up the issue of the Transportation 
Element at its meeting on June 18, 
1985. 
Following its 
deliberations on the Transportation Element, the revised draft Core 
Area Plan will be the subject of another public hearing. 
After that 
final hearing, the City Council will either revise the plan further 
or adopt it.

III. QUESTION PRESENTED. 
May Council Member Hall participate in the City Council's 
deliberations and decision on the Transportation Elernent of the Core 
Area Plan, but abstain from the Council's deliberations and decision 
on the remaining elements and sections of the Core Area Plan? 
IV. 
DISCUSSION. 
The Core Area Plan recommended by the Planning Commission 
perceived land use and transportation policies as one related issue, 
with transportation as the controlling issue. 
It was my view, and 
Council Member Hall's, that this analytical framework prevented him 
from acting on the Transportation Element: 
even though that element 
does not directly affect his interests, its policies were used to 
determine the land use and urban design policies which did (and 
still do) affect his financial interests. 
The City Council's method of addressing the Core Area Plan has, 
in essence, reversed the hierarchy of policies established by the 
Planning Commission: 
al though land use, 
urban design and 
transportation are still viewed as related issues, the participating 
Council Members viewed land use and urban design as controlling, and 
transportation policy as derivative of those elements. 
There is now reason to ask, therefore, whether Council Member 
Hall may participate in the City Council's deliberations and 
decisions on the Transportation Element of the Core Area Plan only. 
The Land Use and Urban Design Elements do have a material financial 
effect on some of Council Member Hall's interests, and we acknow-
ledge further that the General Plan must comprise " ... an integrated, 
internally consistent statement of policies •.. II 
(Government Code 
§65300.5). 
Land use and urban design policies, however, appear to 
be set, though not yet formally adopted. 
The policies in the Trans-
portation Element, on the other hand, do not appear to have a 
similar impact on Council Member Hall's interests and, if they do, 
they may well be indistinguishable from their effects on the public 
generally. 
Therefore, due to the local and regional significance of 
transportation policy, and the absence of a direct effect on Council 
Member Hall's interests, we hope for a favorable response on his 
limited participation.

THE CORE AREA PLAN 
Development in the Core Area over the past eight years has

(A copy of the Core Area Plan is attached and marked "Exhibit C.") 
Adopted in 1975, the Core Area Plan is the general plan for the

(See Government Code §§65300 -
65302 for a description 
of the purpose of the general plan and the subjects that must be 
addressed in the plan). 
By state law, every city is required to 
adopt a general plan which sets forth the development policies in 
the area which it covers (Government Code §65300). 
As such, the 
general plan holds a fundamental place in a city's land use 
policies. 
Its fundamental nature is strengthened by the fact that 
all other planning decisions of consequence must be consistent with 
the general plan (Government Code §§ 65860 and 65910). 
Among the policies set forth in the 1975 Core Area Plan is 
that its time frame should be limited to ten years: 
Unlike the other parts of the General Plan, 
this Core Area Plan has been designed to 
direct the City's development for only the 
next ten years. 
Because of the great uncer-
tainties surrounding the nation's economic 
condition, possible fuel shortages, environ-
mental regulations, and the like, it was felt 
that the City could not realistically plan 
for the Core Area any more than ten years in 
advance •••• 
Core Area Plan, p. 7

III. DISCUSSIQN 
There are facts presented in this request which distinguish it 
from earlier FPPC opinions, and which suggest that Councilman Hall 
should be allowed to participate in the Core Area Plan revision 
process and the Town Centre project. 
As to the Core Area. Plan: 
The mere fact that the Core Area Plan is being revised does 
not make it "reasonably foreseeable" that Councilman Hall's 
interests will be affected. 
This request is importantly different 
from the issue presented in Thorner, 1 FPPC 198 (1975). 
There, 
Director McPhail of the Marin Municipal water District was required 
to abstain from all discussions relating to lifting a moratorium 
against water hook-ups. 
The rationale was that lifting the mora-
torium would in itself generate an increase in building activity, 
creating a conflict for the Director who owned a building supply 
company. 
No such direct connection is presented here between the 
revisions to the plan and Councilman Hall's interests. 
Until the 
various studies are completed and a draft plan prepared, it is not 
possible to state with certainty whether his interests will be af-
fected, or how they might be affected. 
If it appears from the 
direction of the revision process that his interests will be 
affected directly or peculiarly, Councilman Hall would abstain at 
that point if a legal conflict exists. 
Even if it is reasonably foreseeable that Councilman Hall's 
interests may be affected, the facts support a conclusion that the 
effect will not be material. 
First, the Mt. Diablo Boulevard pro-
perty which Councilman Hall owns is underdeveloped under the exist-
ing Core Area Plan and zoning regulations. 
This property could 
potentially be developed to six stories for office use. 
It is 
therefore unlikely that revisions to the Core Area Plan would sig-
nificantly affect the value of this property to any greater extent 
than it is affected by existing policies. 
Second, over the next 
ten years, the value of the two properties which Councilman Hall 
owns in the Core Area is more likely to be directly affected by 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983 
Page 9 
other factors: 
in the case of the Mt. Diablo Boulevard property, 
it is subject to a lease which, if its options are exercised by the 
lessee, will not terminate until 1991; in the case of the Creekside 
Drive property, it is possible that the state will widen I-680 at 
that point, resulting in a partial or total condemnation of the 
property. 
Finally, unless the Core Area Plan revises the policies 
which apply directly to his property, the materiality of the effect 
of the revisions on Councilman Hall's interests cannot be easily 
(or inexpensively) determined. 
As the Commission noted in GillmQt, 
3 FPPC 38 (1977), Section 18702(b) of the FPPC regulations is not a 
reliable gUide to measuring the external effects of land use poli-
cies (3 FPPC at 42, fn. 4). 
Although I understand that there are 
land use economists who can analyze and quantify the effects of 
such policies, such a study would be time-consuming and costly. 
Apart from the issues of foreseeability and materiality, the 
facts presented support a conclusion that the effect (if any) on 
Councilman Hall's interests will be "indistinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally." The size of the Walnut Creek Core 
Area, and the type and intensity of development which has occurred 
within it, suggest that it should be viewed as a "significant seg-
ment of the public." Compared to the 23-square block core area at 
issue in ~, 2 FPPC 79 (1976), the Walnut Creek Core Area is both 
significantly larger and more intensely developed. 
In contrast to 
the downtowp commercial district discussed in Btown, 4 FPPC 19 
(1978), the Walnut Creek Core Area represents a large percentage of 
the office development in the City as a whole (66%). 
In relation 
to the entire Core Area, Councilman Hall's two properties within 
the Area (Mt. Diablo Boulevard and Creekside Drive) represent .13 
percent of the acreage, .41 percent of the office floor area, and 
.1 percent of the apartments. 
Given the nature of the Core Area, 
and the size of Councilman Hall's interests in relation to it, a 
basis exists for concluding that Councilman Hall's interests are 
indistinguishable from those of a significant segment of the 
public. 
Similar considerations apply regarding Councilman Hall's 
participation in Town Centre: 
A question exists whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that 
the proposed project will have an effect on Councilman Hall's 
interests. 
Unlike the situation presented in OglesQy, 1 FPPC 71 
(1975), Councilman Hall does not own property in the project area. 
His situation also differs from that presented in GillmQt, SUptg. 
In that case, Mayor Gillmor owned real property approximately 100 
yards away from a proposed senior citizens project. 
His property, 
however, was primarily devoted to retail uses, including his own 
restaurant, which could foreseeably benefit from new housing in the 
area. 
Councilman Hall's Mt. 
Diablo Boulevard property is admit-
tedly close to the proposed Town Centre project, but it is develop-
ed for office purposes and leased to a commercial tenant. 
Thus the 
direct connection be"tween the project and the financial interests 
which existed in OglesQy and GillmQr is appreciably weaker here.

A. 
THE CORE AREA PLAN 
Development in the Core Area over the past eight years has 
proceeded under policies set forth in the City's "Core Area Plan." 
(A copy of the Core Area Plan is attached and marked "Exhibit C.") 
Adopted in 1975, the Core Area Plan is the general plan for the 
Core Area. 
(See Government Code §§65300 -
65302 for a description 
of the purpose of the general plan and the subjects that must be 
addressed in the plan). 
By state law, every city is required to 
adopt a general plan which sets forth the development policies in 
the area which it covers (Government Code §65300). 
As such, the 
general plan holds a fundamental place in a city's land use 
policies. 
Its fundamental nature is strengthened by the fact that 
all other planning decisions of consequence must be consistent with 
the general plan (Government Code §§ 65860 and 65910). 
Among the policies set forth in the 1975 Core Area Plan is 
that its time frame should be limited to ten years: 
Unlike the other parts of the General Plan, 
this Core Area Plan has been designed to 
direct the City's development for only the 
next ten years. 
Because of the great uncer-
tainties surrounding the nation's economic 
condition, possible fuel shortages, environ-
mental regulations, and the like, it was felt 
that the City could not realistically plan 
for the Core Area any more than ten years in 
advance •••• 
Core Area Plan, p. 7 
Consistent with that policy, in April, 1983, the City Council 
directed the staff to begin a review of the Core Area Plan and its 
policies. 
The entire process is expected to take about 18 months, 
with proposed revisions to be submitted to the City Council in 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
August, 1984, and scheduled for hearings in September, 1984. 
The 
City Council will then make the final decision on any revisions to 
the plan. 
The process of revising the Core Area Plan requires the 
involvement and coordination of various decision-makers. 
(A copy 
of the "1983-84 Core Area Plan Revision Work Program -
Time Line" 
is attached and marked "Exhibit D.") For example, the City's Trans-
portation Commission will be primarily responsible for reviewing 
data related to traffic and circulation issues; the City's Design 
Review Commission will be primarily responsible for reviewing the 
Urban Design Element of the Plan; and the Planning Commission is 
primarily responsible for the Plan's Land Use Element. 
The Plan-
ning Commission is also the body charged with the responsibility of 
supervising and coordinating the process, and making recommenda-
tions to the City Council on the proposed revisions. 
Generally speaking, the Plan will be prepared in separate 
phases: 
First, planning staff will collect data pertaining to the 
Core Area: 
existing use and intensity data, traffic information, 
market and other economic data, and census data. 
Second, this data 
will be analyzed by staff and by economic and .transportation con-
sultants in an attempt to answer various factual and policy ques-
tions, such as: 
What is the market demand for various types of 
development in the Core Area over the next 10 years? 
What is the 
Core Area's physical capacity for development? 
What improvements, 
if any, are needed to extend that capacity? 
What are the costs and 
revenues associated with various types and levels of development? 
At the same time, staff will be working with the Planning Commis-
sion to develop planning policy alternatives. 
These alternatives 
will deal with land use issues and related matters. 
When the data 
analysis is completed and the policy alternatives have been review-
ed, staff will prepare a draft Core Area Plan which, in its judg-
ment, addresses the various issues in a manner consistent with the 
community's goals and values. 
The draft plan will then go through the steps which are legal-
ly required for adoption. 
An environmental impact report will be 
done which evaluates the anticipated environmental effects of the 
plan. 
If it appears that the plan will have a significant effect 
on the environment, the EIR must propose alternatives which will 
diminish or eliminate those effects. 
By state law, the Planning 
Commission is required to hold at least one public hearing on the 
proposed plan; by its own policy, however, the Commission will hold 
additional public hearings, particularly when alternative develop-
ment scenarios are being explored. 
Following the public hearing 
process, the Planning Commission will act on the environmental im-
pact report, and make its recommendations to the City Council on 
revisions to the Core Area Plan. 
The City Council will then hold 
at least one public hearing on the proposed revisions and environ-
mental documentation, and either accept, reject, or modify the Com-
mission's recommendations.

III. DISCUSSIQN 
There are facts presented in this request which distinguish it 
from earlier FPPC opinions, and which suggest that Councilman Hall 
should be allowed to participate in the Core Area Plan revision 
process and the Town Centre project. 
As to the Core Area. Plan: 
The mere fact that the Core Area Plan is being revised does 
not make it "reasonably foreseeable" that Councilman Hall's 
interests will be affected. 
This request is importantly different 
from the issue presented in Thorner, 1 FPPC 198 (1975). 
There, 
Director McPhail of the Marin Municipal water District was required 
to abstain from all discussions relating to lifting a moratorium 
against water hook-ups. 
The rationale was that lifting the mora-
torium would in itself generate an increase in building activity, 
creating a conflict for the Director who owned a building supply 
company. 
No such direct connection is presented here between the 
revisions to the plan and Councilman Hall's interests. 
Until the 
various studies are completed and a draft plan prepared, it is not 
possible to state with certainty whether his interests will be af-
fected, or how they might be affected. 
If it appears from the 
direction of the revision process that his interests will be 
affected directly or peculiarly, Councilman Hall would abstain at 
that point if a legal conflict exists. 
Even if it is reasonably foreseeable that Councilman Hall's 
interests may be affected, the facts support a conclusion that the 
effect will not be material. 
First, the Mt. Diablo Boulevard pro-
perty which Councilman Hall owns is underdeveloped under the exist-
ing Core Area Plan and zoning regulations. 
This property could 
potentially be developed to six stories for office use. 
It is 
therefore unlikely that revisions to the Core Area Plan would sig-
nificantly affect the value of this property to any greater extent 
than it is affected by existing policies. 
Second, over the next 
ten years, the value of the two properties which Councilman Hall 
owns in the Core Area is more likely to be directly affected by 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
other factors: 
in the case of the Mt. Diablo Boulevard property, 
it is subject to a lease which, if its options are exercised by the 
lessee, will not terminate until 1991; in the case of the Creekside 
Drive property, it is possible that the state will widen I-680 at 
that point, resulting in a partial or total condemnation of the 
property. 
Finally, unless the Core Area Plan revises the policies 
which apply directly to his property, the materiality of the effect 
of the revisions on Councilman Hall's interests cannot be easily 
(or inexpensively) determined. 
As the Commission noted in GillmQt, 
3 FPPC 38 (1977), Section 18702(b) of the FPPC regulations is not a 
reliable gUide to measuring the external effects of land use poli-
cies (3 FPPC at 42, fn. 4). 
Although I understand that there are 
land use economists who can analyze and quantify the effects of 
such policies, such a study would be time-consuming and costly. 
Apart from the issues of foreseeability and materiality, the 
facts presented support a conclusion that the effect (if any) on 
Councilman Hall's interests will be "indistinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally." The size of the Walnut Creek Core 
Area, and the type and intensity of development which has occurred 
within it, suggest that it should be viewed as a "significant seg-
ment of the public." Compared to the 23-square block core area at 
issue in ~, 2 FPPC 79 (1976), the Walnut Creek Core Area is both 
significantly larger and more intensely developed. 
In contrast to 
the downtowp commercial district discussed in Btown, 4 FPPC 19 
(1978), the Walnut Creek Core Area represents a large percentage of 
the office development in the City as a whole (66%). 
In relation 
to the entire Core Area, Councilman Hall's two properties within 
the Area (Mt. Diablo Boulevard and Creekside Drive) represent .13 
percent of the acreage, .41 percent of the office floor area, and 
.1 percent of the apartments. 
Given the nature of the Core Area, 
and the size of Councilman Hall's interests in relation to it, a 
basis exists for concluding that Councilman Hall's interests are 
indistinguishable from those of a significant segment of the 
public. 
Similar considerations apply regarding Councilman Hall's 
participation in Town Centre: 
A question exists whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that 
the proposed project will have an effect on Councilman Hall's 
interests. 
Unlike the situation presented in OglesQy, 1 FPPC 71 
(1975), Councilman Hall does not own property in the project area. 
His situation also differs from that presented in GillmQt, SUptg. 
In that case, Mayor Gillmor owned real property approximately 100 
yards away from a proposed senior citizens project. 
His property, 
however, was primarily devoted to retail uses, including his own 
restaurant, which could foreseeably benefit from new housing in the 
area. 
Councilman Hall's Mt. 
Diablo Boulevard property is admit-
tedly close to the proposed Town Centre project, but it is develop-
ed for office purposes and leased to a commercial tenant. 
Thus the 
direct connection be"tween the project and the financial interests 
which existed in OglesQy and GillmQr is appreciably weaker here.

and the

The proposed Plan consists of five related

A land use element;

A transportation element;

An implementation sectioni and

Following is a brief discussion of the function and significant 
provisions of each section of the proposed Plan. 
1. 
Land Use Element. 
Together with the land use maps, this element specifies the 
permitted use, and permitted intensity of development, of every 
parcel of land in the Core Area. 
The proposed Plan would change the 
permitted uses on many parcels. 
It would also impose, for the first 
time, a floor-area ratio (FAR) on commercial parcels to control the 
intensity of permissible development (Figure 5). 
The floor-area 
ratio of any parcel is the ratio of building floor area to net land 
area t 
both expressed in square feet. 
For a 30,000 square- foot 
parcel, for example an FAR of 1.0 would permit a building floor area 
of 30,000 square feet; an FAR of .5 would allow a building floor 
area of 15,000 square feet; and an FAR of 1.5 would permit a 45,000 
square-foot building. 
Generally speaking, the proposed Plan permits 
less development throughout the Core Area than would have been 
permitted under the 1975 Plan.

Planning staff there-
fore identified relationships between development capacity generally 
in the Core Area, and the transportation system improvements neces-
sary to maintain adequate service levels when such development

Even if no development were permitted, however, traffic 
experts predicted that service levels at some intersections would

occurring outside the city limits. 
With this background, the Transportation Element calls for 
various measures to maintain and improve pedestrian, bicycle and 
vehicle circulation in the Core Area. 
Central to the element is a 
set of recownended street improvements which range in significance

and the construction of new ones (Figure 10). 
The most significant 
debate at the Planning Commission concerned the proper method to 
relieve peak hour congestion on Ygnacio Valley Road, the City's 
major east-west arterial. 
Staff recommended that westbound traffic 
on Ygnacio Valley Road in the Core Area be diverted to a new one-way 
street to be constructed along a new right-of-way; the parallel 
section of ygnacio Valley Road would then be converted to a one-way 
street for eastbound traffic only, with the two streets forming a 
one-way "couplet." 
This approach was rejected by a majority of the 
Planning Commission. 
In place of the couplet, the Plan recommends 
widening of Ygnacio Valley Road to eight lanes in the downtown area. 
Although Ygnacio Valley Road is the most significant 
transportation improvement, I should emphasize that the Plan calls 
for many other street improvements and changes

sections.conclusion_synthetic: For the Core Area Plan revision, Mr. Hall need not disqualify himself at the initial approval stage, but must disqualify himself from future decisions as revisions are developed. For the Town Centre rezoning and DDA decisions, Mr. Hall must disqualify himself due to material financial effects on his Mt. Diablo Boulevard property, Travelers Insurance rental income, and potentially Newell Hill Shopping Center management fee, with the "public generally" exception unlikely to apply given the localized nature of the project's effects.

percent of the office space in the entire City: 2 million square

17 acres of park and open space; and 70 acres of institutional 
uses/public buildings (schools and hospitals). 
(An 11" x 17" map 
showing the distribution of existing land uses in the Core Area is

demand for office development in the Core Area has been particular-
ly strong. 
In 1982 and 1983, the City approved 1.7 million square

Market research by the City staff indicates that there is 
a demand for 500,000 square feet of new office development per year 
over the next 10 years, a demand that could, for the most part, be 
accommodated under existing policies. 
A. 
THE CORE AREA PLAN 
Development in the Core Area over the past eight years has 
proceeded under policies set forth in the City's "Core Area Plan." 
(A copy of the Core Area Plan is attached and marked "Exhibit C.") 
Adopted in 1975, the Core Area Plan is the general plan for the 
Core Area. 
(See Government Code §§65300 -
65302 for a description 
of the purpose of the general plan and the subjects that must be 
addressed in the plan). 
By state law, every city is required to 
adopt a general plan which sets forth the development policies in 
the area which it covers (Government Code §65300). 
As such, the 
general plan holds a fundamental place in a city's land use 
policies. 
Its fundamental nature is strengthened by the fact that 
all other planning decisions of consequence must be consistent with 
the general plan (Government Code §§ 65860 and 65910). 
Among the policies set forth in the 1975 Core Area Plan is 
that its time frame should be limited to ten years: 
Unlike the other parts of the General Plan, 
this Core Area Plan has been designed to 
direct the City's development for only the 
next ten years. 
Because of the great uncer-
tainties surrounding the nation's economic 
condition, possible fuel shortages, environ-
mental regulations, and the like, it was felt 
that the City could not realistically plan 
for the Core Area any more than ten years in 
advance •••• 
Core Area Plan, p. 7 
Consistent with that policy, in April, 1983, the City Council 
directed the staff to begin a review of the Core Area Plan and its 
policies. 
The entire process is expected to take about 18 months, 
with proposed revisions to be submitted to the City Council in 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
August, 1984, and scheduled for hearings in September, 1984. 
The 
City Council will then make the final decision on any revisions to 
the plan. 
The process of revising the Core Area Plan requires the 
involvement and coordination of various decision-makers. 
(A copy 
of the "1983-84 Core Area Plan Revision Work Program -
Time Line" 
is attached and marked "Exhibit D.") For example, the City's Trans-
portation Commission will be primarily responsible for reviewing 
data related to traffic and circulation issues; the City's Design 
Review Commission will be primarily responsible for reviewing the 
Urban Design Element of the Plan; and the Planning Commission is 
primarily responsible for the Plan's Land Use Element. 
The Plan-
ning Commission is also the body charged with the responsibility of 
supervising and coordinating the process, and making recommenda-
tions to the City Council on the proposed revisions. 
Generally speaking, the Plan will be prepared in separate 
phases: 
First, planning staff will collect data pertaining to the 
Core Area: 
existing use and intensity data, traffic information, 
market and other economic data, and census data. 
Second, this data 
will be analyzed by staff and by economic and .transportation con-
sultants in an attempt to answer various factual and policy ques-
tions, such as: 
What is the market demand for various types of 
development in the Core Area over the next 10 years? 
What is the 
Core Area's physical capacity for development? 
What improvements, 
if any, are needed to extend that capacity? 
What are the costs and 
revenues associated with various types and levels of development? 
At the same time, staff will be working with the Planning Commis-
sion to develop planning policy alternatives. 
These alternatives 
will deal with land use issues and related matters. 
When the data 
analysis is completed and the policy alternatives have been review-
ed, staff will prepare a draft Core Area Plan which, in its judg-
ment, addresses the various issues in a manner consistent with the 
community's goals and values. 
The draft plan will then go through the steps which are legal-
ly required for adoption. 
An environmental impact report will be 
done which evaluates the anticipated environmental effects of the 
plan. 
If it appears that the plan will have a significant effect 
on the environment, the EIR must propose alternatives which will 
diminish or eliminate those effects. 
By state law, the Planning 
Commission is required to hold at least one public hearing on the 
proposed plan; by its own policy, however, the Commission will hold 
additional public hearings, particularly when alternative develop-
ment scenarios are being explored. 
Following the public hearing 
process, the Planning Commission will act on the environmental im-
pact report, and make its recommendations to the City Council on 
revisions to the Core Area Plan. 
The City Council will then hold 
at least one public hearing on the proposed revisions and environ-
mental documentation, and either accept, reject, or modify the Com-
mission's recommendations. 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
Throughout the process, the staff will keep the City Council 
informed of the plan's progress and direction. 
Periodic "brief-
ings" will be scheduled at Council meetings, at which time Council

posed project is outlined in orange on Exhibit A.) Consistent with

velopment Plan-Mt. Diablo Redevelopment Project," is attached and

tail blocks to the north on Main and Locust Streets.

exceeds $100,000. 
(d) 
Councilman Hall owns the real property at 3011 
Ludell Court in the City of Pleasant Hill (.19 acre). 
It is 
located about 1 mile north of the City's boundary and 1.3 miles 
north of the Core Area. 
It is improved with a single-family home 
and rented. 
The property is listed for sale through the multiple 
listing service. 
The value of this property exceeds $100,000. 
3. 
Councilman Hall, individually or through his ownership 
of Dynamic Agents, Inc., has the. following sources of income: 
(a) 
He receives income for his personal services from 
Dynamic Agents, Inc. 
The value received is greater than $10,000 
per year. 
(b) 
He receives rental income and a property manage-
ment fee from 1821-1825 Mt. Diablo Boulevard. 
The value received 
is greater than $10,000 per year. 
{c} 
If the conveyance of 1335 Creekside Drive is 
accomplished, Councilman Hall will receive rental income and a 
management fee from the property. 
The projected value will exceed 
$10,000 per year. 
(d) 
In January, 1983, Dynamic Agents, Inc. took over 
as the professional manager of Newell Hill Center, a 22-tenant re-
tail shopping center. 
For its services, it receives a fee based on 
a percentage of the gross amounts collected from the lessees (pri-
marily rent). 
(Newell Hill Center is identified in green on Exhib-
it A.) The value received is greater than $10,000 per year. 
The 
property is listed for sale through the multiple listing service, 
and negotiations have been held with a potential buyer for about 
six months. 
Although a contract of sale has not been entered into, 
the sale could be consummated as early as February, 1984. 
If the 
buyer completes the sale, it will assume the professional manage-
ment responsibilities, and the interest of Dynamic Agents, Inc. 
will end. 
(e) 
Councilman Hall receives rental income, and Dy-
namic Agents, Inc. 
receives a property management fee, from the 
property at 3011 Ludell Court. 
The value received is greater than 
$10,000. 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
(f) 
He receives rental income and a property manage-
ment fee for the property at 2780 and 2790 Jones Road. 
The value 
received is greater than $10,000 per year. 
4. 
Councilman Hall is a director and officeholder in 
Dynamic Agents, Inc. 
II. 
OUESTIONS fRESENTED 
A. 
As to the Core Area Plan revisions: 
1. 
Under the facts stated above, is Councilman Hall "par-
ticipating in the making of a governmental decision" within the 
meaning of Government Code §87100 and §18700(c) of the FPPC Regu-
lations?l/ 
2. 
If Councilman Hall's activities amount to "participa-
tion," is it "reasonably foreseeable" within the meaning of Govern-
ment Code §87100 and §18702 of the FPPC Regulations that his par-
ticipation will affect his financial interests? 
3. 
If it is "reasonably foreseeable" that his interests 
will be affected, will that effect be "material" within the meaning 
of Government Code §87103 and §18702 of the FPPC Regulations? 
4. 
If the answers to questions A(l), (2) and (3) are af-
firmative, can Councilman Hall participate nevertheless on the 
theory that the effect on his interests is "indistinguishable from 
the effect on the public generally" within the meaning of Govern-
ment Code §87103 and §18703 of the FPPC Regulations? 
B. 
As to the rezoning of certain property for the proposed 
Town Centre redevelopment project: 
1. 
Is it "reasonably foreseeable" that the proposed 
rezoning will affect Councilman Hall's interests? 
2. 
If so, will that effect be material? 
3. 
If the answers to B(l) and (2) are in the 
affirmative, can Councilman Hall participate nevertheless on the 
theory that the effect on his interests is indistinguishable from 
the effect on the public generally? 
liThe effect of the proposed Core Area Plan on Councilman Hall's 
interests is speculative at this time, because the proposed Plan 
has not been written and will not be prepared until February, 1984. 
At this time, therefore, I am not asking whether Councilman Hall 
can vote on the adoption of the Plan. 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
C. 
As to the proposed DDA for the Town Centre redevelopment 
project: 
1. 
Is it "reasonably foreseeable" that the proposed 
DDA will affect Councilman Hall's financial interests? 
2. 
If so, will that effect be material? 
3. 
If the answers to C(l) and (2) are in the affirmative, 
can Councilman Hall participate nevertheless on the theory that the 
effect on his interests is indistinguishable from the effect on the 
public generally? 
III. DISCUSSIQN 
There are facts presented in this request which distinguish it 
from earlier FPPC opinions, and which suggest that Councilman Hall 
should be allowed to participate in the Core Area Plan revision 
process and the Town Centre project. 
As to the Core Area. Plan: 
The mere fact that the Core Area Plan is being revised does 
not make it "reasonably foreseeable" that Councilman Hall's 
interests will be affected. 
This request is importantly different 
from the issue presented in Thorner, 1 FPPC 198 (1975). 
There, 
Director McPhail of the Marin Municipal water District was required 
to abstain from all discussions relating to lifting a moratorium 
against water hook-ups. 
The rationale was that lifting the mora-
torium would in itself generate an increase in building activity, 
creating a conflict for the Director who owned a building supply 
company. 
No such direct connection is presented here between the 
revisions to the plan and Councilman Hall's interests. 
Until the 
various studies are completed and a draft plan prepared, it is not 
possible to state with certainty whether his interests will be af-
fected, or how they might be affected. 
If it appears from the 
direction of the revision process that his interests will be 
affected directly or peculiarly, Councilman Hall would abstain at 
that point if a legal conflict exists. 
Even if it is reasonably foreseeable that Councilman Hall's 
interests may be affected, the facts support a conclusion that the 
effect will not be material. 
First, the Mt. Diablo Boulevard pro-
perty which Councilman Hall owns is underdeveloped under the exist-
ing Core Area Plan and zoning regulations. 
This property could 
potentially be developed to six stories for office use. 
It is 
therefore unlikely that revisions to the Core Area Plan would sig-
nificantly affect the value of this property to any greater extent 
than it is affected by existing policies. 
Second, over the next 
ten years, the value of the two properties which Councilman Hall 
owns in the Core Area is more likely to be directly affected by 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
other factors: 
in the case of the Mt. Diablo Boulevard property, 
it is subject to a lease which, if its options are exercised by the 
lessee, will not terminate until 1991; in the case of the Creekside 
Drive property, it is possible that the state will widen I-680 at 
that point, resulting in a partial or total condemnation of the 
property. 
Finally, unless the Core Area Plan revises the policies 
which apply directly to his property, the materiality of the effect 
of the revisions on Councilman Hall's interests cannot be easily 
(or inexpensively) determined. 
As the Commission noted in GillmQt, 
3 FPPC 38 (1977), Section 18702(b) of the FPPC regulations is not a 
reliable gUide to measuring the external effects of land use poli-
cies (3 FPPC at 42, fn. 4). 
Although I understand that there are 
land use economists who can analyze and quantify the effects of 
such policies, such a study would be time-consuming and costly. 
Apart from the issues of foreseeability and materiality, the 
facts presented support a conclusion that the effect (if any) on 
Councilman Hall's interests will be "indistinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally." The size of the Walnut Creek Core 
Area, and the type and intensity of development which has occurred 
within it, suggest that it should be viewed as a "significant seg-
ment of the public." Compared to the 23-square block core area at 
issue in ~, 2 FPPC 79 (1976), the Walnut Creek Core Area is both 
significantly larger and more intensely developed. 
In contrast to 
the downtowp commercial district discussed in Btown, 4 FPPC 19 
(1978), the Walnut Creek Core Area represents a large percentage of 
the office development in the City as a whole (66%). 
In relation 
to the entire Core Area, Councilman Hall's two properties within 
the Area (Mt. Diablo Boulevard and Creekside Drive) represent .13 
percent of the acreage, .41 percent of the office floor area, and 
.1 percent of the apartments. 
Given the nature of the Core Area, 
and the size of Councilman Hall's interests in relation to it, a 
basis exists for concluding that Councilman Hall's interests are 
indistinguishable from those of a significant segment of the 
public. 
Similar considerations apply regarding Councilman Hall's 
participation in Town Centre: 
A question exists whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that 
the proposed project will have an effect on Councilman Hall's 
interests. 
Unlike the situation presented in OglesQy, 1 FPPC 71 
(1975), Councilman Hall does not own property in the project area. 
His situation also differs from that presented in GillmQt, SUptg. 
In that case, Mayor Gillmor owned real property approximately 100 
yards away from a proposed senior citizens project. 
His property, 
however, was primarily devoted to retail uses, including his own 
restaurant, which could foreseeably benefit from new housing in the 
area. 
Councilman Hall's Mt. 
Diablo Boulevard property is admit-
tedly close to the proposed Town Centre project, but it is develop-
ed for office purposes and leased to a commercial tenant. 
Thus the 
direct connection be"tween the project and the financial interests 
which existed in OglesQy and GillmQr is appreciably weaker here. 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
November 28, 1983  
It is weakened even further with respect to Councilman Hall's in-
terests in Newell Hill Center and Ludell Court, as his interests 
will end with the expected sale of both properties. 
If it is "reasonably foreseeable" that Councilman Hall's 
interests may be affected, it is questionable whether the effect 
will be material. 
The same considerations apply here which I 
discussed above in relation to the Core Area Plan. 
Lastly, there is a basis for concluding that Town Centre's 
effect (if any) on Councilman Hall's interests is indistinguishable 
from its affect on the public generally. 
The discussion on this 
issue above applies with equal or greater force to Town Centre. 
If 
there is a basis for concluding that the Core Area represents a 
"significant segment of the public" (and I have argued that there 
is), Town Centre will affect Councilman Hall's interests in sub-
stantially the same manner as it affects other property owners in 
the Core Area. 
Thank you for your analysis of the questions presented in this 
request. 
If you need any additional information, I would be 
pleased to provide it to you.

CONCLUSION: For the Core Area Plan revision, Mr. Hall need not disqualify himself at the initial approval stage, but must disqualify himself from future decisions as revisions are developed. For the Town Centre rezoning and DDA decisions, Mr. Hall must disqualify himself due to material financial effects on his Mt. Diablo Boulevard property, Travelers Insurance rental income, and potentially Newell Hill Shopping Center management fee, with the "public generally" exception unlikely to apply given the localized nature of the project's effects.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter 83A266 addresses conflict of interest issues for Walnut Creek City Councilman Merle Hall regarding his participation in decisions on a Core Area Plan revision and a Town Centre redevelopment project, analyzing his various real property holdings and income sources against Government Code Section 87100 requirements. The FPPC advises that Mr. Hall must disqualify himself from most decisions regarding the Town Centre project due to material financial effects on his nearby property and business interests.
